Summary of what was found: Metsä Board shows that folding boxboard is a core secondary-packaging material in beautycare and is used for cartons as well as premium decorative effects.
Detailed description of what was found: This source supports folding boxboard as a mainstream secondary-packaging substrate for cosmetics and personal care. It states that beautycare folding boxboards are suitable for multi-layer embossing, foil laminations, holograms, 3D special effects, high-quality printing on both sides, and sturdy packaging that creates an unboxing experience. For later lightweighting work, it helps distinguish standard lightweight paperboard use from premium paperboard use with added decorative treatments that can increase material intensity.
Source name: Beautycare — Paperboard end uses — Metsä Board — https://www.metsagroup.com/metsaboard/products-and-services/paperboard-applications/beautycare/

Summary of what was found: Stora Enso establishes folding boxboard as a mainstream material base for cosmetics and personal care while showing lighter-weight FBB as a market direction.
Detailed description of what was found: This source supports the baseline for secondary packs built from folding carton board. It identifies Performa as a premium folding-boxboard family and describes Performa Lumi as a GC1 folding boxboard designed for premium visual impact and efficient material use, while also noting that the platform is robust yet lightweight. It also shows broader folding-carton options including luxury-board and brown-fiber board families, which is useful for comparing mass-market cartons, premium cartons, and material-efficient luxury-board options.
Source name: Premium folding carton boards for packaging — Stora Enso — https://www.storaenso.com/en/products/paperboard-materials/folding-cartons

Summary of what was found: Stora Enso provides an explicit SBS baseline for luxe beauty packaging and ties SBS to demanding finishes and tactile premium effects.
Detailed description of what was found: This source identifies solid bleached sulfate as a luxury-board portfolio for high-end packaging, including cosmetics and perfumes. It says SBS boards are engineered for premium folding cartons and high-end packs, provides coated and uncoated SBS examples, and says these boards tolerate embossing, foil stamping, metallized foils, and other demanding finishing treatments while maintaining stiffness and sharp folds. This is strong evidence that premium/luxe beauty often uses high-spec SBS for secondary cartons when tactile feel, whiteness, print quality, and decorative finishing are central.
Source name: Coated and uncoated carton boards for luxury packaging — Stora Enso — https://www.storaenso.com/en/products/paperboard-materials/folding-cartons/carton-boards-for-luxury-packaging

Summary of what was found: Smurfit Westrock shows that paperboard secondary packaging includes boxes, trays, and carton sleeves, not only standard tuck cartons.
Detailed description of what was found: This source supports mapping paperboard-based secondary formats. It states that folding cartons are part of a broader paperboard packaging portfolio that includes carton sleeves and multipack solutions, and that folding-carton systems can be delivered in different styles such as boxes and trays. It also notes that folding cartons can carry varnishes, foil-type embellishments, textured effects, window patches, and a range of board grades, making it useful for mapping mainstream and upgraded secondary formats before lightweighting evaluation.

Summary of what was found: Smurfit Westrock confirms coated recycled board as a current paperboard option for folding cartons and custom applications.
Detailed description of what was found: This source supports the baseline for recycled paperboard in secondary beauty and personal-care packaging. It states that coated recycled board is made with recycled fibers and has a smooth clay coating that supports printability for folding cartons and custom applications. This is useful as a mass-market and scale-oriented baseline because it is paper-based, scalable, and often more aligned with lower-cost secondary packaging than rigid-box constructions.

Summary of what was found: WestRock provides a coated natural kraft paperboard baseline for secondary packaging.
Detailed description of what was found: The source identifies coated natural kraft paperboard as a current board option and states that it delivers crisp graphics while retaining the natural-kraft substrate character. For baseline purposes, CNK is relevant where brands want a natural or rustic premium look without moving to much heavier rigid formats. It should be included as a paperboard alternative to SBS and CRB when appearance, strength, and material positioning differ.

Summary of what was found: GPA Global’s Sugarpill case shows a premium/luxe rigid-box construction using rigid greyboard, decorative outer wrap, film treatment, foil, magnets, and ribbon.
Detailed description of what was found: This is strong evidence for the premium/luxe secondary-packaging baseline. The case states that the pack used a rigid greyboard structure, wrapped exterior paper, soft-touch film coating, foils, spot UV, concealed magnets, and a ribbon lift. For later lightweighting work, this type of pack is a material-intensive target because the structural board, decorative wrap, closure hardware, and embellishments all layer material and weight onto a non-primary beauty package.

Summary of what was found: Smurfit Westrock says rigid boxes are often used for luxury packaging and that choosing a lower board weight can reduce cost and material use.
Detailed description of what was found: This source directly supports the premium-versus-mass split. It states that paperboard comes in various weights and that choosing a lower weight reduces cost, while also noting that rigid boxes are often used for luxury packaging and that their quality brings a higher price point. This is useful evidence for why mass-market packs tend to favor lighter board and why later lightweighting work should treat board-weight optimization and rigid-to-folding-carton substitution as important material-based pathways.
Source name: Ways to adjust your luxury packaging to meet your business goals — Smurfit Westrock — https://www.smurfitwestrock.com/blog/ways-to-adjust-your-luxury-packaging-to-meet-your-business-goals

Summary of what was found: Fedrigoni provides a direct material taxonomy for labels: paper or plastic-film facestock, adhesive layer, and release liner.
Detailed description of what was found: This source supports the label baseline for secondary and tertiary packaging where pressure-sensitive labels are used on cartons, sleeves, gift packs, shippers, and related formats. It states that self-adhesive labelstock has three main components and that the printed facestock can be paper or plastic film, including PP, PE, PET, and PVC. This is useful because labels can add mixed-material complexity and affect recyclability when filmic constructions or heavy laminations are used.
Source name: Self-adhesive material: what it is and how it is made — Fedrigoni — https://fedrigoni.com/en/news/self-adhesive-material-what-it-is-and-how-it-is-made/

Summary of what was found: CCL confirms shrink sleeves as a current beauty and personal-care packaging format and signals polyolefin sleeve development for recycling compatibility.
Detailed description of what was found: This source supports the baseline for sleeve materials in beauty packaging. The product page states that beauty and personal-care shrink sleeves provide 360-degree decoration, confirming their continued use as high-graphics sleeve formats. CCL’s beauty-market information also references floatable polyolefin shrink sleeves that support PET recycling, helping distinguish conventional plastic sleeve systems from improved sleeve-material options that may reduce recycling interference.
Source name: Beauty & Personal Care Shrink Sleeves — CCL Label — https://ccllabel.com/product/beauty-personal-care-shrink-sleeves/

Summary of what was found: GPA Global’s molded-pulp guide provides a replacement pathway for plastic trays and inserts in premium cosmetics: molded pulp made from recycled paper or natural fibers.
Detailed description of what was found: This source is central for replacing plastic inserts and trays. It states that molded pulp can be made from recycled paper or natural fibers, lists inputs such as bagasse, bamboo, wheat straw, old newsprint, OCC, and paper, and identifies cosmetics applications including interior cushioning, liner trays, and exterior/secondary packaging. It also addresses the premium/luxe challenge by noting advances in finish, color matching, and surface quality, making molded pulp viable beyond basic brown transit applications.
Source name: Our guide to moulded pulp packaging — GPA Global — https://www.gpaglobal.net/sustainability/our-guide-moulded-pulp-packaging-luxury-cosmetics/

Summary of what was found: Smurfit Westrock identifies foam and plastic trays as historic defaults for delicate premium products and names corrugate and molded fiber as paper-based replacement routes.
Detailed description of what was found: This source supports why premium packs often become material-intensive and what replacement pathways exist for plastic or foam inserts. It states that foam or plastic trays were historically go-to protective materials for delicate products, but corrugate and molded fiber inserts are increasingly preferred as brands push for sustainability. This identifies premium internal fitments as a high-priority target area for lightweighting and material substitution.
Source name: Ways to Make Your Luxury Packaging More Sustainable — Smurfit Westrock — https://www.smurfitwestrock.com/blog/ways-to-make-your-luxury-packaging-more-sustainable

Summary of what was found: Smurfit Westrock establishes corrugated paper-fiber shipping boxes as the mainstream tertiary baseline for cosmetics, skincare, haircare, and wellness.
Detailed description of what was found: This source supports the tertiary-packaging baseline for corrugated shippers used in direct-to-consumer and e-commerce channels. It states that health-and-beauty shipping boxes are made from paper fiber, are designed to be recyclable, protect fragile cosmetics and skincare in transit, and can be delivered flat-packed in strong yet lightweight designs. This is a key baseline combination for later comparison: corrugated paper-fiber shipper plus optional internal paper-based protection.
Source name: Health & Beauty Shipping Boxes — Smurfit Westrock — https://www.smurfitwestrock.com/products/packaging/ecommerce/health-beauty-shipping-boxes

Summary of what was found: Smurfit Westrock’s FaleLokiKoki case provides a cosmetics-specific example of replacing assorted fillers and plastic materials with a single corrugated insert.
Detailed description of what was found: This direct beauty-sector customer case explains that a Polish distributor of cosmetics and hairdressing products replaced bulk packaging with assorted fillers by adopting a single-piece corrugated insert that divided the shipper into multiple sections. It reports lower damage complaints, faster packing, and elimination of adhesive tape, foil, and plastic fillers. This is highly relevant proof that corrugated inserts are a practical tertiary-packaging replacement pathway in beauty e-commerce.
Source name: FALELOKIKOKI — Reducing damage complaints by 75% with a corrugated packaging insert — Smurfit Westrock — https://www.smurfitwestrock.com/innovation/customer-stories/falelokikoki

Summary of what was found: Paptic provides a paper-like wood-fibre mailer option positioned for e-commerce and premium unboxing.
Detailed description of what was found: This source supports current material options for mailers in tertiary packaging. It states that Paptic mailers are designed for e-commerce and emphasizes performance, functionality, and a soft premium feel for the unboxing experience. It sits between conventional corrugated boxes and traditional paper or plastic mailers, making it a relevant lightweighting alternative where brands want lower-bulk mailer formats without defaulting to plastic.

Summary of what was found: Pregis establishes paper-based cushioned mailers as a current e-commerce protective-packaging format.
Detailed description of what was found: Pregis states that the EverTec mailer uses durable two-layer paper construction, resilient cushioning, double-walled kraft paper, and curbside recyclability. This documents a mailer format that combines protection and reduced pack size relative to a box. It is a useful baseline for lightweight, low-cube, paper-based tertiary packaging that can replace some box-plus-void-fill combinations for smaller cosmetic and personal-care orders.

Summary of what was found: Sealed Air confirms inflated plastic-film pillows as a mainstream void-fill material in e-commerce tertiary packaging.
Detailed description of what was found: Sealed Air states that inflatable air pillows are used to prevent shifting, bruising, and breakage during transit, and that they function as efficient void-fill solutions shipped and stored flat. This identifies a material class that is lightweight in shipped mass but still fossil-film-based and should be evaluated against paper-based alternatives in replacement or recyclability workstreams.

Summary of what was found: Ranpak shows paper-based cushioning and paper-based void fill as scalable current protective-packaging materials for shipping applications.
Detailed description of what was found: Ranpak describes PadPak paper as a cushioning material for fragile products and describes its void-fill solutions as paper-based dunnage used to fill empty space and immobilize products inside shipping boxes. These sources represent already-commercial, scalable, recyclable paper-based protection systems that can be benchmarked against bubble, foam, and air-pillow systems.
Source name: Paper Cushioning Solutions; Sustainable Void Fill Solutions — Ranpak — https://www.ranpak.com/cushioning/ ; https://www.ranpak.com/void-fill/
